coredata.dc:description: In recent years, construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on the in-situ fluorescence reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) is more convenient, simple and efficient for sensing various targets, which has attracted widespread attention from researchers. However, there is still a lack of systematic summary of its design strategies and applications, which may prevent its further development in this field. In this review, we will focus on the construction and application of ratiometric probes based on the in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD. In addition, construction strategies of ratiometric fluorescent probes are divided into three main categories according to the different generated fluorescent products via in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD. The first category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is based on the yellow fluorescence product. The second category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is based on the blue fluorescence product. The third category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is combined blue with yellow fluorescent products mentioned-above. Meanwhile, the multiple applications of the three types ratiometric fluorescent probes are summarized. This will provide us in-depth idea for the multiple-function development of ratiometric fluorescent probes in terms of the in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD, and promote its research in broader fields in the future.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probes based on fluorogenic reactions of o-phenylenediamine for multiple sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi, 046011, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi, 046011, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on the in-situ fluorescence reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) is more convenient, simple and efficient for sensing various targets, which has attracted widespread attention from researchers. However, there is still a lack of systematic summary of its design strategies and applications, which may prevent its further development in this field. In this review, we will focus on the construction and application of ratiometric probes based on the in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD. In addition, construction strategies of ratiometric fluorescent probes are divided into three main categories according to the different generated fluorescent products via in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD. The first category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is based on the yellow fluorescence product. The second category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is based on the blue fluorescence product. The third category of construction ratiometric fluorescent probe is combined blue with yellow fluorescent products mentioned-above. Meanwhile, the multiple applications of the three types ratiometric fluorescent probes are summarized. This will provide us in-depth idea for the multiple-function development of ratiometric fluorescent probes in terms of the in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD, and promote its research in broader fields in the future.

Scheme 1. Illustration of two categories in-situ fluorogenic reactions of OPD: (A) Oxidation reaction of OPD with the generation of orange-yellow fluorescent product of 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP or oxOPD); (B) Condensation reaction of OPD with the formation of blue fluorescent product of 3-(dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxaline (DFQ or OXD).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the three different fluorescence detection modes of “turn-on”, “turn-off” and “ratiometric”, ratiometric fluorescence detection technique has stand out for the various quantitation analytes. Different from the “turn-on” or “turn-off” mode, which is relying on the change of a single fluorescence emission signal and is susceptible to the instruments or external environment [1–3], the “ratiometric” mode can avoid the background interference, enhance the accuracy and expand the detection range [4–9] due to the dependence of the ratio of two fluorescence intensities as readout signals. Up to date, multitude efforts have been devoted to constructing the “ratiometric” sensors. Unfortunately, the common strategy for designing ratiometric fluorescent probes is based on hybrid of two different fluorescent materials, which process is usually time-consuming, complicated and high-cost, hindering its wider application in the realm of sensing [10]. Thus, the exploration of more convenient and more simple ratiometric fluorescence methods is becoming more and more urgent. Fortunately, in-situ fluorogenic reactions just by simple mixing reagents has advantages of time-saving operation over nanohybrids. In recent years, in-situ fluorogenic reaction of OPD has sparked significant research interest to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes. OPD has two adjacent amino groups and OPD itself is no fluorescence. However, after interaction with different chemical substances [11], different color fluorescence heterocyclic compounds can be gained.

Now, we classify the in-situ fluorogenic reactions of OPD into the following two categories according to the color of the fluorescence products (Scheme 1): (1) generation of orange-yellow fluorescent 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP/oxOPD) via oxidation reactions [12–14]; (2) formation of blue luminescent 3-(dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxaline (DFQ/OXD) via condensation reactions [15–17]. By combining such unique fluorescence response with other chromophores, more and more ratiometric fluorescence probes have been developed for sensing various analytes, such as nanosheets [18], carbon quantum dots [19–22], metal nanoclusters [23–27], etc, as well as organic luminous materials [28,29]. However, the research is still in its infancy. With the aim of achieving more sensitive, simple and time-efficient detection, it is urgently needed to summarize the design strategies of in-situ fluorogenic of OPD in ratiometric sensing, which will play an integral role for versatile applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Based yellow fluorescence products of OPD for ratiometric fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: OPD itself is non-fluorescent. However, OPD can be easily oxidized to yield a yellow fluorescent substance 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) by some metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Ce4+) [30–32], some oxides (H2O2, KMnO4) [33,34], natural oxidase, and peroxidase-mimicking nanozymes [35–38], as illustrated in the left part of Scheme 1 . Thus, the first type of ratiometric fluorescent probes designing strategy is successfully explored based on incorporation of luminescent materials with the unique fluorescence reaction of OPD together (method 1). Since the fluorescence emission peak of DAP is located approximately at 557 nm, the luminescent materials with a distance of 100 nm from the emission peak of DAP becomes the best candidates such as carbon quantum dots (CDs), metal nano-clusters (MNCs), metal nanoparticles (MNPs), etc, to build ratiometric fluorescent probes more effectively. In the past decade, a variety of analytes like metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Hg2+, etc), enzymes, pesticides, organic molecules, and so on, have been successfully detected based on this method. Now we summarize and elaborate as follows.

Heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Ag+ can accumulate in the human body along with the ecological circulation system, and then react with proteins and enzymes, resulting a serious threat to human health, such as neurodegenerative diseases and metabolic disorders [39,40]. It is extremely significant to track trace level of such heavy metal ion sensitively and selectively by ratiometric fluorescent probes. Method 1 is the one way to achieve this goal and have been widely used to sense metal ions.

According to Method 1, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is a perfect candidate to be used as reference fluorophore in designing ratiometric fluorescence probes for sensing metal ions. Using blue-emission of N-CDs as reference fluorophore, fluorogenic reactions of o-phenylenediamine and Cu2+ as response signal. Zhang et al. [41] designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to determine Cu2+ (Scheme 2 ). In this sensing method, OPD is firstly oxidized by Cu2+ to yield oxOPD, a yellow fluorescence substance with emission peak at 553 nm. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of oxOPD increased remarkably with the increasing concentration of Cu2+. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 412 nm was quenched progressively by the generated oxOPD by virtue of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). As a result, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F553/F412) was directly proportional to the varied content of Cu2+ in the range of 0.08–10 μM. Furthermore, it is satisfactory to obtain the low limit of detection (LOD) of 23 nM towards Cu2+. Using the similar sensing mechanism of the above-mentioned example, Ma et al. [42] exploited a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to mornitor Cu2+ in the rat brain. In brief, OPD was oxidized to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) by Cu2+, then, the fluorescence of C-ssDNA was quenched by oxOPD at 452 nm based on FRET under the π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. Thus, a sensory strategy for sensing Cu2+ was successfully realized.

In addition to metal ions (Cu2+, Ag+), some bio-enzymes (laccase) [43] and artificial enzymes (nanoparticles, CDs, etc) [44–46] in the presence of O2 can also catalyze the oxidation of OPD to form the yellow fluorescence oxOPD. However, Hg2+ is extremely unfriend to this type of enzymatic reaction, hindering the oxidation process of OPD to become oxOPD strikingly. By coupling of such enzyme inhibition property and other emissions like metal nanoclusters, Si quantum dots (SiQDs), etc, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for Hg2+ is rationally proposed. Li et al. [43] reported a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay for sensing Hg2+ via combining laccase (LACC) inhibition and l-proline protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) as dual emission signals (Scheme 3 ). oxOPD was produced relying on the oxidation reaction of OPD and LACC, with emission peak at 576 nm. However, the absorption of as-resulted oxOPD was overlapped with the emission bands of AuNCs. Consequently, the blue-fluorescence of AuNCs at 419 nm was quenched on the basis of inner filter effect (IFE). Whereas, the presence of Hg2+ inhibited the oxidation reaction efficiently and in turn depressed the IFE effect effectively. Then, the fluorescence of AuNCs was recovered while that of oxOPD was decreased. As a result, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two emissions was sensibly applied as the yardstick for Hg2+ detection. Furthermore, a low limit detection of 0.27 μM towards Hg2+ was obtained and its practical feasibility in tap water and Yangtze river water was successfully performed.

More recently, considering the poor stability of biological enzymes [47], nanozymes such as platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) [44], graphitic carbon nitride (C3N4) [46], and so on, have been widely employed as substitutes for bio-enzymes to conquer the issue. For instance, Li et al. [45] synthesized PtNPs as nano nano-enzyme to catalyze oxidation OPD for the generation of oxOPD in the presence of H2O2 (Scheme 4 ). Following a similar ratiometric fluorescence detection strategy, the sensing system of SiQDs/PtNPs/OPD/H2O2 as dual signals was proposed to detect Hg2+. As to this sensor, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs at 414 nm was effectively quenched owing to FRET between oxOPD and SiQDs. However, with the addition of Hg2+, the catalytic behavior of PtNPs was depressed, and further prevented the production of oxOPD, resulting the fluorescence intensity of oxOPD decreased at 576 nm, while the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs changed inversely because of the suppressed FRET effect between oxOPD and SiQDs. Thus, the ratio of I576/I414 served a sensitive response signal to sense Hg2+, with a low detection limit of 10.5 nM.

It is well known that biological enzymes in human bodies play critical roles for human health, such as alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [48–50], acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [51], and so on. Abnormal levels of these enzymes in serum often result in various diseases, like liver dysfunction [52], diabetes [53], bone disease [54], Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, prostate cancer [55] originated from high levels of ALP, and Huntington's disease attributed to low level of AChE. Therefore, rapid and efficient detection of these enzymes is becoming extremely urgent.

Due to the multiple functionalities and tunable catalytic activities of nanozymes, it is a more absorbing strategy to design ratiometric fluorescent probes for versatile analytes by coupling in-situ fluorogenic reaction of OPD under the catalysis of nanozymes with various luminescent materials, as have been mentioned-above. In this sensing strategy, MnO2 sheets, as a novel-dimensional (2D) nanomaterial, are frequently employed as the preferred nanozymes to detect biomacromolecules (ALP, AChE) owing to the superior properties of including high stability, facile synthesis, redox property, peroxidase-like activity, excellent biocompatibility and wide absorption band (300–600 nm) [56–58]. On one hand, the wide absorption band of MnO2 nanosheets endows them with strong fluorescence quenching capability toward most kinds of fluorescent materials [59] based on IFE or FRET mechanism. On the other hand, the intermediate valence of Mn4+ endows with strong oxidation and catalytic activities, which can easily oxidize OPD to yellow fluorescence oxOPD. Therefore, using MnO2 nanosheets in combination with other luminous materials has been widely exploited for constructing ratiometric fluorescence probes [60,61].

In 2020, Ye et al. [62] designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect AchE successfully (Scheme 5 ) by using fluorescent substance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and MnO2 nanosheets. Based on IFE effect, GQDs with blue emission peak at 450 nm is efficiently quenched. Meanwhile, on the basis of catalytic oxidation function of MnO2 nanosheets, non-fluorescent of OPD changes to yellow fluorescent substance of oxOPD peaked at 572 nm. However, with the appearance of AChE and the substrate of acetylthiocholine (ATCh), hydrolysis product of thiocholine (TCh) is produced, which suppress both catalytic oxidation activity and fluorescence quenching ability of MnO2 nanosheets. As a result, fluorescence signal at 450 nm of GQD enhanced but reduced at 572 nm. Consequently, the ratiometric fluorescence signals of F
450
/F
572 is recorded to determine AchE. Furthermore, serum samples and the screening of inhibitors had been successfully performed.

ATP (adenosine triphosphate), as an indispensable energy complex, is highly linked with cellular activity like DNA synthesis, neurotransmission, and triphosphoric acid cycle [63–65]. Monitoring its concentration is becoming a significant thesis. In 2021, Wu et al. [66] reported a ratiometric fluorescence assay for ATP detection based on Cu2+-triggered oxidation of OPD with CDs assembly (Scheme 6 ). In the absence of ATP, redox reaction between OPD and Cu2+ occurs and forms a yellow fluorescence product of DAP, accompanied by quenching the fluorescence of CDs via IFE. However, when ATP is present, it will compete with OPD to coordinate with Cu2+ inhibiting the generation of DAP. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs restores owing to the diminution of IFE between CDs and DAP, making satisfactory ratiometric quantification of ATP come into being. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative detection of ATP is realized by the naked eyes.

Wang et al. [67] designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe via redox reaction of OPD and KMnO4 in cooperation with blue emissive carbon dots (CDs) to trace ascorbic acid (AA) and ascorbic acid oxidas (AA-Ox) (Scheme 7 ). Under the oxidation of KMnO4, OPD is rapidly transformed to oxOPD along with the appearance of a new emission peak at 565 nm. In parallel, fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm of CDs decreases attributed to the FRET between CDs and oxOPD. Thus, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I565/I450) is changed as the concentration of AA changes.

In 2021, Cao et al. [56] designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to determine glutathione (GSH) based on nanosheets (NS), CDs and OPD (Scheme 8 ). Herein, NS played triple critical roles. First, NS can be used as an efficient quencher to reduce the fluorescence of CDs due to its wide absorption band. Second, NS can oxidize OPD to generate DAP with yellow fluorescence emission centered at 570 nm owing to its oxidase-like property. Third, NS can react with GSH after introducing GSH into the sensing system, leading to the fluorescence recovery of CDs and quenching of DPA. Correspondingly, the recorded ratio of fluorescence intensity (I570/I465) can be used to indicate the concentration of GSH.

Alendronate sodium (ALDS), as a common third-generation bisphosphonate medicine, is regularly used to treat bone diseases like osteoporosis and hypercalcemia [68,69]. However, excessive ALDS can cause toxicity to soft tissues [70]. To achieve sensitive detection of ALDS, Xia et al. [71] designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combined with Ce4+ (Scheme 9 ). Ce4+ shows a potent oxidase-like behavior and is naturally capable of oxidizing OPD into OXD directly without the aid of H2O2. Concurrently, the as-produced OXD can quench the fluorescence of GQDs at 444 nm significantly based on IFE because of the overlap of the absorption spectrum between GQDs and OXD. Besides, Ce4+ can also oxidize ALDS owing to its powerful oxidative ability. Thus, after addition of ALDS, the fluorogenic reactions of OPD and Ce4+ is inhibited. As a result, fluorescence intensity of ALDS decrease while that of GQDs increase with the increasing concentration of ALDS. Consequently, sensitive detection of ALDS was realized with low detection limit of 33 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Based blue fluorescence products of OPD for ratiometric fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: It is extremely amazing that OPD can not only produce the above-mentioned yellow fluorescence product, but also can yield the blue fluorescence substance by condensation reaction. Dicarbonyl group of dehydro-ascorbic acid (DHAA) can undergo condensation reaction with diamine group of OPD to form strong blue fluorescence quinoxaline (QX) derivative [15–17,72]. Taking advantage of this feature of OPD, the second tactic for constructing ratio fluorescent probes has emerged accordingly. Additionally, DHAA can be attained by the redox reaction of AA and some oxidants, such as metal ions (Fe3+, Ce4+, Cu2+, Ag+, etc), norit, 2,6-dichloroindoindophenol, N-bromosuccinimide and iodone, etc [73,74]. In this regard, it is an intelligent strategy to build ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for extensive analytes based blue fluorescence product of OPD in conjunction with other luminous materials.

In 2020, Han et al. [75] selected the orange-red fluorescence mesoporous silica nanoparticle-gold nanoclusters (MSN-AuNCs) as reference material. Meanwhile, in-situ fluorescence reaction of DHAA and OPD was applied as response signal (Scheme 10 ) to conduct ratiometric detection of ALP. This sensing work involves the following procedures: (1) Substrate 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid (AAP) of ALP is hydrolysed into AA. (2) AA is converted into DHAA when Fe3+ is in the present. (3) Condensation reaction between dicarbonyl group of DHAA and diamine group of OPD occurs to form QX. Fluorescence intensity of MSN-AuNCs is slightly changed in the above three processes.

While, fluorescence intensity of QX is varied dependent on the concentration of ALP. Thus, ratiometric determination of ALP is available, exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, with a low detection limit of 0.1 U/L. Additionally, ALP detection in human serum samples is successfully conducted. Later, by replacing metal nanoclusters with yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (YNCD) and replacing Fe3+ with Cu2+, Lv et al. [76] also developed a ratiometric fluorometric assay for ALP based on the similar methodology. Meanwhile, the considerable limit of detection with 0.14 U/L of this sensor is gained.

In 2024, Wang et al. [1] designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AA detection. In this ratiometric measurement, a fluorophore of Mn doped ZnS QDs (Mn: ZnS QDs) and in-situ fluorescence reaction of DHAA and OPD were combined together for dual fluorescence signals output (Scheme 11 ). Herein, DHAA is a product of the redox reaction of Fe3+ and AA, which can react with OPD to yield a fluorescent substance. When Mn: ZnS QDs were added in, two emission peaks appeared at 434 nm and 590 nm, respectively. On one hand, with the increasing concentration of AA, the fluorescence intensity of Mn: ZnS QDs decreased gradually in view of more and more formation of Fe2+. On the other hand, fluorescence intensity at 434 nm increased gradually owing to the incremental content of DFQ. As a result, the ratio of F434/F590 was employed to determine AA quantitatively. Two linear range of 0.5–40 μM and 40–80 μM were obtained, with low detection limit of 0.21 μM.

Recently, our group [77] proposed a novel strategy for ratiometric determination of o-phenylenediamine via combination of in-situ fluorogenic reaction and in-situ generation of fluorescent substances (Scheme 12 ). Herein, Cu2+ acts as both an oxidizing agent and a precursor. As an oxidizing agent, Cu2+ firstly reacts with AA to fabricate DHAA. Subsequently, the resulted DHAA reacted with OPD to form blue fluorescent OXD peaked at 434 nm, which can be used as a response signals. As a precursor, the residual Cu2+ reacts with dithiothreitol (DTT) for production of red-emitting dithiothreitol-copper nanoparticles (DTT-CuNPs), which can be applied as reference signals. As a result, ratiometric detection of OPD was successfully realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ensembled two colors fluorescent products of OPD into a ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, the usage of nanohybrids for ratiometric fluorescence determination often is commonly complex and consuming. Encouragingly, OPD can produce fluorescence products with two different colors, which provides new techniques for designing ratiometric fluorescent detection. Thus, the third method for ratiometric fluorescence probes based on two kinds homogeneous chemical reaction of OPD has been put forward. Now, we summarize as follows.

In 2022, Wang et al. [79] constructed an effective ratiometric fluorescent platform for AA detection (Scheme 14 ). Dual in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD and dual mimic nanozymatic activity of copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) were intelligently combined in this sensing work. On one hand, CuO NPs with ascorbate oxidase mimetic property can catalyze AA to DHAA in the presence of O2. Subsequently, in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD and DHAA occurs to form a blue fluorescent product of 3-(1, 2- dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ). On the other hand, CuO NPs also exhibit oxidase activity in the presence O2, leading catalytic oxidation in-situ fluorescence reaction of OPD becomes possible and generates a yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD). Thus, the above two types in-situ fluorescence reactions of OPD becomes competitive by feat of the introduction of AA. Thus, ratios of such two fluorescent signals are attained to indicate the content of AA. Furthermore, determination of AA in practical samples of human serum and fruits is realized with satisfactory recovery rates.

Xia et al. [80] strategically designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect alendronate (ALDS) with Ce4+-triggered cascade chromogenic reaction (Scheme 15 ). It involves three steps: (1) Ce4+ can oxidize AA to produce DHAA, which is followed by condensation reaction with OPD to generate a blue emissive product with emission peak at 434 nm, named m3-(dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ). (2) Ce4+ can also be used as oxidase-mimics to directly react with OPD to produce yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with emission peak at 568 nm. (3) The introduction of ALDS takes Ce4+ and prevents Ce4+ from reacting with OPD, thus resulting fluorescence intensity at 568 nm decreases. Accordingly, a ratiometric sensing assay is successfully realized via an effective “mix-then-test” way.

In addition to the two categories of OPD-based fluorogenic reactions described above, diazotization reactions of OPD and nitrite (NO2 −) is another favorable type of in-situ fluorogenic reaction for building efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensors. By feat of such diazotization reaction, Hao et al. [81] rationally designed a dual-signal ratiometric approach to detect NO2 − by using nanozymes N-CDs as luminescent materials (Scheme 16 ). Firstly, OPD and NO2 − react to form a diazo compound of benzotriazole (BTA) under acidic conditions. After a few minutes of diazotization, the solution is adjusted to a slight alkaline condition with the pH range of 7.0–9.0, followed by the activation of yellow fluorescence of BTA. Herein, N-CDs exhibits photoluminescence and photo-responsive oxidase-like activity. With the formation of BTA, IFE between BTA and N-CDs appears, resulting in the quenching activity of N-CDs and generating a ratiometric fluorescence response.

In this paper, we mainly reviewed the design strategies and sensing applications of the ratiometric fluorescent probes based on in-situ fluorogenic reactions of OPD. OPD can react with various chemical regents to yield blue or yellow fluorescence products, so ratiometric fluorescent probes are ingeniously designed for detecting various analytes. In the design strategies, various luminescent materials like metal nanoclusters (MNCs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (QDs) and other materials have been significantly used in combination with the in-situ fluorogenic reactions of OPD. Additionally, it is a more efficient way to design ratiometric fluorescent probes according to different fluorescent products via different in-situ fluorescence responses of OPD. Although significant progress has been made, more improvements and discoveries still need to be attempted to expand the application areas, such as for the biological analysis and optical imaging applications. We firmly believe that this review will play an integral role in the advancement of ratiometric fluorescent methods based on different types of in-situ fluorogenic reaction of OPD for versatile applications.

Jingxiang Shen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoxia Wang: Investigation. Li Qin: Supervision, Investigation. Long Sun: Supervision. YaQin Liang: Supervision, Investigation. Rui Li: Data curation. Haiquan Tian: Data curation. Xinlu Zhang: Data curation.
